title: Defining COM Components
text: Defining COM Components To define COM components, make the appropriate definitions in the Signature Editor. Within the Signature Editor, define a component with COM as its Implementation. See Define a Component Signature . Specify the information about the component’s operations and their parameters. In the Define COM Properties form, specify the ProgID or CLSID of the COM object that implements the service. In the Define Parameter form, specify the parameter attributes for each operation. Pay particular attention to the Data Type setting. The availability of other fields, such as Interface or Literal Name , depends on the data type for the particular parameter. The value of the Literal Name field is stored in the COM Interface DD when it is created, and is available to COM applications that use these services. A Uniface occurrence is mapped to COM’s ADOR. Recordset type with a single or number of occurrences. If you want to map a COM data type to a Uniface data type, you should select the appropriate Interface from the drop-down list: If you want to pass a number of occurrences of an entity to a COM service, you should define the parameter as having data type Entity. If you need to pass a single occurrence of an entity as a parameter to a COM service, select the Occurrence data type for the parameter. Related Links COM and Uniface Parameter Data Types Converting Uniface parameter data types to and from the COM environment has both a static and dynamic nature. When you export a COM Interface DLL, the data types of the signature definitions must be matched to the appropriate COM types, taking into account the user’s implementation selections. At runtime, both Uniface and COM allow the user to pass variants for parameters, so the connector can dynamically convert data types, within reason. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
